## II. Two Kinds of Disagreement

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META:** Conceptual core, taught once, flowing out of the §I cases. Frame vs fact + the diagnostic. Axes kept light — named as examples, not belabored; we concede they're obvious and make the narrower point. Cruxes detailed in §IV. Keep emphasis sparing.
:::

The cases above look like one argument, but they are two different kinds of disagreement wearing the same clothes, and pulling them apart is most of the work.

Some disagreements are about the world. Does power-seeking emerge from capable optimization? Is oversight tractable in the time we have? Will a qualitatively dangerous system even arrive? These have a fact of the matter; evidence bears on them, and in principle they can be settled. Call them **cruxes** — they are the high-leverage nodes of the [interactive tool](https://lifeuniversesafety.com/doom-assumptions/index.html), the probabilities where worldviews diverge.

Other disagreements are not about the world but about the question being asked. What counts as "doom"? Over what horizon? How much does current model behavior weigh against a structural prior? There is nothing to settle here — these are framing choices, and they decide what would even count as evidence. Call them **axes** — they are the labels on the tool: the danger, the timeframe, the inside-or-outside view you set before any number means anything.

None of that is a discovery. Everyone knows the timeframe matters and that "doom" is slippery. The useful point is narrower: because two people can set these quietly and differently, their p(doom) figures can be incomparable even when stated to the decimal, and no evidence closes the gap until the labels match. That gives an order of operations for a stuck argument. First check whether you share a frame; if you don't, that is the disagreement, and trading evidence is wasted motion. Only once the frame is shared is it worth finding the crux, because that is the part evidence can move. Hinton and LeCun never shared an evidentiary standard, which is why a decade of progress moved neither toward the other. Bengio and Yudkowsky share everything except one crux, which is why their disagreement is sharp enough to state in a sentence.

The rest of the map is three things. The **spine** is the mechanism — the branching tree from the first two posts: does AI raise the danger at all, through one dominant system or many, does that system model what it is doing, does it expect the catastrophe it causes. The gallery in §III runs along it. The axes are the framing parameters just named — definition of doom, time horizon, evidentiary standard — carried to the whole tree. And the cruxes, the factual bets where movement happens, wait for §IV, where they read more clearly against the positions they move between.
